instruction: What are best practices for C++ header file design? Show include guards, forward declarations, and the PIMPL idiom for compile-time isolation.
output:
<think>
C++ header design directly affects compilation speed, binary compatibility, and API clarity. The key issues:

1. Include guards vs #pragma once: Both prevent multiple inclusion. #pragma once is non-standard but universally supported and less error-prone. Include guards are portable and standard.

2. Forward declarations: Declare a type without including its header when you only need pointers/references. This reduces header dependencies and compilation time dramatically.

3. PIMPL (Pointer to Implementation): Hide implementation details behind an opaque pointer. Benefits: (a) changing implementation doesn't recompile users, (b) binary compatibility across versions, (c) faster compilation.

4. Include-what-you-use (IWYU): Only include headers you directly use. Don't rely on transitive includes.

The compilation cost issue: in a large codebase, a header included by 1000 files means any change recompiles 1000 files. PIMPL + forward declarations reduce this fan-out dramatically.

I'll show a realistic example: a database connection class using PIMPL to hide the actual database library headers from users.
</think>

```cpp
// === database.h — Public header (what users include) ===

#ifndef MYLIB_DATABASE_H  // Include guard (standard, portable)
#define MYLIB_DATABASE_H

// Alternative: #pragma once (non-standard but universally supported)

#include <memory>      // For std::unique_ptr
#include <string>      // For std::string (used in public API)
#include <string_view> // For std::string_view
#include <vector>      // For std::vector (used in public API)
#include <optional>    // For std::optional

// Forward declarations — avoid including heavy headers
// Users only need pointers/references to these
namespace mylib {

class Transaction;  // Forward declare — defined in transaction.h
struct QueryResult; // Forward declare — defined in query_result.h

// PIMPL: hide all implementation details
class Database {
public:
    // Constructor/destructor MUST be declared here, defined in .cpp
    // (because unique_ptr needs complete type for deletion)
    explicit Database(std::string_view connection_string);
    ~Database();

    // Move-only (can't copy a unique_ptr)
    Database(Database&& other) noexcept;
    Database& operator=(Database&& other) noexcept;
    Database(const Database&) = delete;
    Database& operator=(const Database&) = delete;

    // Public API — no implementation details leak
    bool connect();
    void disconnect();
    [[nodiscard]] bool is_connected() const;

    [[nodiscard]] std::optional<QueryResult>
    query(std::string_view sql);

    [[nodiscard]] std::vector<QueryResult>
    query_all(std::string_view sql);

    [[nodiscard]] Transaction begin_transaction();

private:
    // PIMPL: opaque pointer hides everything
    struct Impl;  // Forward-declared nested class
    std::unique_ptr<Impl> impl_;
};

// Public result type — keep it simple
struct QueryResult {
    std::vector<std::string> columns;
    std::vector<std::vector<std::string>> rows;
    int affected_rows = 0;
};

} // namespace mylib

#endif // MYLIB_DATABASE_H


// === database.cpp — Implementation (users never see this) ===

#include "database.h"
#include "transaction.h"

// Heavy implementation headers — only compiled in this .cpp
#include <libpq-fe.h>     // PostgreSQL C API
#include <mutex>
#include <iostream>

namespace mylib {

// Full implementation class — completely hidden from users
struct Database::Impl {
    std::string connection_string;
    PGconn* conn = nullptr;  // PostgreSQL handle
    std::mutex mtx;
    bool connected = false;

    explicit Impl(std::string_view cs)
        : connection_string(cs) {}

    ~Impl() {
        if (conn) {
            PQfinish(conn);
        }
    }

    bool do_connect() {
        conn = PQconnectdb(connection_string.c_str());
        connected = (PQstatus(conn) == CONNECTION_OK);
        return connected;
    }

    std::optional<QueryResult> execute(std::string_view sql) {
        std::lock_guard lock(mtx);
        PGresult* res = PQexec(conn, std::string(sql).c_str());
        if (PQresultStatus(res) != PGRES_TUPLES_OK) {
            PQclear(res);
            return std::nullopt;
        }
        QueryResult qr;
        int ncols = PQnfields(res);
        for (int c = 0; c < ncols; ++c) {
            qr.columns.push_back(PQfname(res, c));
        }
        int nrows = PQntuples(res);
        for (int r = 0; r < nrows; ++r) {
            std::vector<std::string> row;
            for (int c = 0; c < ncols; ++c) {
                row.push_back(PQgetvalue(res, r, c));
            }
            qr.rows.push_back(std::move(row));
        }
        qr.affected_rows = nrows;
        PQclear(res);
        return qr;
    }
};

// Delegate everything to Impl
Database::Database(std::string_view cs)
    : impl_(std::make_unique<Impl>(cs)) {}

Database::~Database() = default;  // unique_ptr handles cleanup
Database::Database(Database&&) noexcept = default;
Database& Database::operator=(Database&&) noexcept = default;

bool Database::connect() { return impl_->do_connect(); }
void Database::disconnect() {
    if (impl_->conn) {
        PQfinish(impl_->conn);
        impl_->conn = nullptr;
        impl_->connected = false;
    }
}
bool Database::is_connected() const { return impl_->connected; }

std::optional<QueryResult> Database::query(std::string_view sql) {
    return impl_->execute(sql);
}

std::vector<QueryResult> Database::query_all(std::string_view sql) {
    auto result = impl_->execute(sql);
    if (result) return {std::move(*result)};
    return {};
}

Transaction Database::begin_transaction() {
    impl_->execute("BEGIN");
    return Transaction(*this);
}

} // namespace mylib

// Benefits achieved:
// 1. Users include database.h — never see libpq-fe.h
// 2. Changing Impl doesn't recompile users
// 3. Adding private members doesn't change ABI
// 4. PostgreSQL can be swapped for MySQL without changing the header
```

instruction: Explain C++20 modules and how they compare to traditional header files. Show a practical module partition example.
output:
<think>
C++20 modules are the modern replacement for the include/header model. Key differences:

1. Headers: textual inclusion (#include copies text), include guards needed, macros leak, compilation order matters, slow (re-parsed in every TU)
2. Modules: compiled once to BMI (Binary Module Interface), no macro leakage, explicit exports, much faster compilation

Module concepts:
- `export module foo;` — declares a module
- `export` keyword controls what's visible to importers
- `import foo;` instead of `#include "foo.h"`
- Module partitions: split a module across files (`module foo:bar;`)
- Implementation partitions: code that's part of the module but not exported

Partitions are like private sub-modules. They allow splitting a large module into logical pieces while presenting a unified API to consumers.

Current state (2025): CMake 3.28+ supports modules with MSVC, GCC 14+, Clang 18+. Adoption is growing but ecosystem support (package managers, build systems) is still maturing.

I'll show a practical networking module with partitions for client, server, and shared types.
</think>

```cpp
// === Traditional header approach (comparison) ===
// #include "net/client.h"  // Textual inclusion, re-parsed every TU
// #include "net/server.h"  // Macros can leak between headers
// #include "net/types.h"   // Include order can matter

// === C++20 Module approach ===

// --- net.types.cppm (module partition: shared types) ---
export module net:types;  // Partition of module 'net'

import <string>;
import <cstdint>;
import <chrono>;

export namespace net {

struct Address {
    std::string host;
    uint16_t port;

    std::string to_string() const {
        return host + ":" + std::to_string(port);
    }
};

enum class Protocol : uint8_t {
    TCP,
    UDP,
    TLS
};

struct Config {
    std::chrono::milliseconds timeout{5000};
    size_t max_connections{100};
    Protocol protocol{Protocol::TCP};
    bool keep_alive{true};
};

// Not exported — visible within module only
struct InternalBuffer {
    char data[4096];
    size_t len = 0;
};

} // namespace net


// --- net.client.cppm (module partition: client) ---
export module net:client;  // Another partition of 'net'

import :types;    // Import the types partition (within same module)
import <memory>;
import <string>;
import <vector>;
import <functional>;

export namespace net {

class Client {
public:
    explicit Client(Address server, Config config = {})
        : server_(std::move(server)), config_(config) {}

    bool connect() {
        // Implementation uses InternalBuffer (module-internal, not exported)
        InternalBuffer buf{};
        // ... TCP connect logic ...
        connected_ = true;
        return connected_;
    }

    void disconnect() { connected_ = false; }

    [[nodiscard]] bool is_connected() const { return connected_; }

    bool send(std::string_view data) {
        if (!connected_) return false;
        // ... send data ...
        return true;
    }

    [[nodiscard]] std::string receive() {
        InternalBuffer buf{};
        // ... receive data ...
        return std::string(buf.data, buf.len);
    }

private:
    Address server_;
    Config config_;
    bool connected_ = false;
};

} // namespace net


// --- net.server.cppm (module partition: server) ---
export module net:server;

import :types;
import <functional>;
import <string>;
import <vector>;
import <thread>;
import <atomic>;

export namespace net {

using ConnectionHandler = std::function<void(std::string_view data)>;

class Server {
public:
    explicit Server(Address bind_addr, Config config = {})
        : bind_(std::move(bind_addr)), config_(config) {}

    ~Server() { stop(); }

    void on_connection(ConnectionHandler handler) {
        handler_ = std::move(handler);
    }

    bool start() {
        running_.store(true);
        // ... bind and listen ...
        return true;
    }

    void stop() {
        running_.store(false);
    }

    [[nodiscard]] bool is_running() const {
        return running_.load();
    }

private:
    Address bind_;
    Config config_;
    std::atomic<bool> running_{false};
    ConnectionHandler handler_;
};

} // namespace net


// --- net.cppm (primary module interface: re-exports partitions) ---
export module net;

// Re-export all partitions
export import :types;
export import :client;
export import :server;

// Module-level convenience functions
export namespace net {

[[nodiscard]] inline Client connect_to(std::string host, uint16_t port) {
    Client c(Address{std::move(host), port});
    c.connect();
    return c;
}

} // namespace net


// --- main.cpp (consumer code) ---
import net;        // Single import gets everything
import <iostream>;

int main() {
    // All types available from single import
    auto client = net::connect_to("localhost", 8080);

    if (client.is_connected()) {
        client.send("hello");
        auto response = client.receive();
        std::cout << response << '\n';
    }

    net::Server server(net::Address{"0.0.0.0", 9090});
    server.on_connection([](std::string_view data) {
        std::cout << "Received: " << data << '\n';
    });
    server.start();

    // InternalBuffer is NOT accessible here — not exported
    // net::InternalBuffer buf;  // Compile error!

    return 0;
}

// === CMakeLists.txt (CMake 3.28+) ===
// cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.28)
// project(netlib CXX)
// set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD 20)
//
// add_library(net)
// target_sources(net PUBLIC FILE_SET CXX_MODULES FILES
//     src/net.types.cppm
//     src/net.client.cppm
//     src/net.server.cppm
//     src/net.cppm
// )
//
// add_executable(app main.cpp)
// target_link_libraries(app PRIVATE net)
```

instruction: Show the forward declaration pattern in C++ to minimize header dependencies. When can you forward-declare and when must you include?
output:
<think>
Forward declarations are crucial for reducing compilation coupling. The rules:

You CAN forward-declare when you only use:
- Pointers to the type (T*)
- References to the type (T&)
- Return types (by pointer/reference)
- Function parameter types (by pointer/reference)
- Member of a smart pointer (unique_ptr<T>, shared_ptr<T> — but unique_ptr needs complete type at destructor)

You MUST include the full header when you:
- Create an instance of the type (need size)
- Inherit from the type (need layout)
- Access members of the type
- Use the type as a value member (need size)
- Call methods on the type
- Use sizeof(T)
- Use in templates that need the complete type

The key insight: the compiler needs the complete type only when it needs to know the size or layout. Pointers are always the same size regardless of what they point to.

I should show a before/after comparison with compilation dependency analysis.
</think>

```cpp
// === BEFORE: Excessive includes (high compilation coupling) ===
// Every change to user.h, order.h, logger.h, or database.h
// forces recompilation of everything that includes service.h

// service_bad.h
#pragma once
#include "user.h"       // Full include
#include "order.h"      // Full include
#include "logger.h"     // Full include
#include "database.h"   // Full include
#include <vector>
#include <string>

class ServiceBad {
    User current_user_;           // Value member: NEEDS full include
    std::vector<Order> orders_;   // Value member: NEEDS full include
    Logger logger_;               // Value member: NEEDS full include
    Database db_;                 // Value member: NEEDS full include
public:
    User get_user(int id);        // Return by value: needs include
    void process(Order order);    // Parameter by value: needs include
};


// === AFTER: Minimal includes with forward declarations ===

// service.h — minimal header dependencies
#pragma once
#include <memory>  // For unique_ptr, shared_ptr
#include <vector>
#include <string>

// Forward declarations — only names, no full definitions needed
class User;
class Order;
class Logger;
class Database;
namespace billing { class Invoice; }  // Forward declare in namespace

class Service {
public:
    Service();   // Defined in .cpp (where Impl is complete)
    ~Service();  // Must be declared — unique_ptr needs complete type at delete

    // Pointers and references: forward declaration is sufficient
    User* find_user(int id);
    const User& get_current_user() const;
    void process_order(const Order& order);
    void set_logger(std::shared_ptr<Logger> logger);

    // Return by value of forward-declared type: NEEDS include in .cpp only
    // (But the CALLER also needs the include to use the result)
    std::unique_ptr<Order> create_order(int user_id);

private:
    // Pointers to forward-declared types: OK
    struct Impl;
    std::unique_ptr<Impl> impl_;  // PIMPL hides value members
};


// service.cpp — HERE we include everything
#include "service.h"
#include "user.h"      // Now we need the full type
#include "order.h"
#include "logger.h"
#include "database.h"

struct Service::Impl {
    User current_user;              // Value member: fine in .cpp
    std::vector<Order> orders;
    std::shared_ptr<Logger> logger;
    Database db;
};

Service::Service() : impl_(std::make_unique<Impl>()) {}
Service::~Service() = default;  // Must be here (Impl is complete)

User* Service::find_user(int id) {
    // Full type available here
    return &impl_->current_user;
}

const User& Service::get_current_user() const {
    return impl_->current_user;
}

void Service::process_order(const Order& order) {
    impl_->orders.push_back(order);  // Needs Order copy constructor
}

std::unique_ptr<Order> Service::create_order(int user_id) {
    return std::make_unique<Order>(user_id);  // Needs Order constructor
}


// === REFERENCE: When forward declaration works ===

class Widget;  // Forward declaration

// ALL of these are fine with just a forward declaration:
class Example {
    Widget* ptr;                     // Pointer member
    Widget& ref;                     // Reference member (unusual but valid)
    std::unique_ptr<Widget> uptr;    // Smart pointer member
    std::shared_ptr<Widget> sptr;    // Smart pointer member
    std::vector<Widget*> ptrs;       // Container of pointers

    void take_ptr(Widget* w);        // Parameter by pointer
    void take_ref(const Widget& w);  // Parameter by reference
    Widget* return_ptr();            // Return pointer
    Widget& return_ref();            // Return reference
    std::unique_ptr<Widget> make();  // Return smart pointer
};

// NONE of these work — need #include "widget.h":
class BadExample {
    // Widget value;                 // ERROR: incomplete type (needs size)
    // std::vector<Widget> vals;     // ERROR: vector needs complete type
    // class Derived : Widget {};    // ERROR: inheritance needs layout
};

void bad_function(Widget w);          // ERROR: by-value param needs size
// sizeof(Widget);                    // ERROR: needs complete type


// === Compilation impact example ===
// With full includes: changing user.h recompiles everything including service.h
//   user.h → service.h → controller.h → app.h → main.cpp (5 files)
//
// With forward decl: changing user.h only recompiles service.cpp
//   user.h → service.cpp (1 file!)
//
// For a 1000-file project, this difference is 10x+ compilation speed.
```

instruction: What is the best way to organize C++ header include order and manage transitive dependencies? Show a practical include policy.
output:
<think>
Include order matters for correctness and maintainability in C++. The key issues:

1. Self-sufficiency: Every header must be compilable on its own (include what you use)
2. Transitive includes: If a.h includes b.h which includes <string>, a.h users get <string> for free — but this is fragile. If b.h changes, a.h users suddenly break.
3. Include order: Convention varies, but Google style (own header first, then C system, then C++ standard, then third-party, then project) catches self-sufficiency bugs.

The "include what you use" (IWYU) principle: Don't rely on transitive includes. If you use std::string, include <string>, even if another header happens to pull it in.

Why own header first: If foo.cpp includes foo.h first, and foo.h forgot to include <string> that it needs, the compilation fails immediately — catching the bug where it should be fixed.

I'll show a practical include policy with rationale and examples of what goes wrong without it.
</think>

```cpp
// === Include order policy ===
// 
// 1. Own header (foo.h in foo.cpp) — catches self-sufficiency bugs
// 2. C system headers (<cstdlib>, <cstring>)
// 3. C++ standard library (<string>, <vector>, <memory>)
// 4. Third-party library headers (<boost/...>, <fmt/...>)
// 5. Project headers ("myproject/...") 
//
// Within each group: alphabetical order.
// Blank line between groups.
// Never use using-directives in headers (using namespace std).

// === order_processor.h — Self-sufficient header ===
#pragma once

// Include everything this header DIRECTLY uses
#include <cstdint>           // uint32_t
#include <functional>        // std::function
#include <memory>            // std::unique_ptr
#include <string>            // std::string
#include <string_view>       // std::string_view
#include <vector>            // std::vector

// Third-party
#include <spdlog/spdlog.h>   // Logger

// Project headers
#include "myproject/types.h" // OrderId, Money

// Forward declarations for types only used by pointer/reference
namespace myproject {
class Database;
class PaymentGateway;
class NotificationService;
}

namespace myproject {

struct OrderItem {
    std::string product_id;
    uint32_t quantity;
    Money price;
};

class OrderProcessor {
public:
    using Callback = std::function<void(OrderId)>;

    OrderProcessor(
        Database& db,
        PaymentGateway& payments,
        NotificationService& notifications
    );
    ~OrderProcessor();

    OrderId submit(std::string_view customer_id,
                   std::vector<OrderItem> items);

    void on_completed(Callback cb);

private:
    struct Impl;
    std::unique_ptr<Impl> impl_;
};

} // namespace myproject


// === order_processor.cpp ===
// OWN HEADER FIRST — catches missing includes in the header
#include "myproject/order_processor.h"

// C system
#include <cassert>

// C++ standard
#include <algorithm>
#include <chrono>
#include <numeric>
#include <stdexcept>

// Third-party
#include <fmt/format.h>

// Project (full includes for forward-declared types)
#include "myproject/database.h"
#include "myproject/notification_service.h"
#include "myproject/payment_gateway.h"

namespace myproject {

struct OrderProcessor::Impl {
    Database& db;
    PaymentGateway& payments;
    NotificationService& notifications;
    Callback on_completed_cb;
};

OrderProcessor::OrderProcessor(
    Database& db,
    PaymentGateway& payments,
    NotificationService& notifications
) : impl_(std::make_unique<Impl>(Impl{db, payments, notifications, nullptr})) {}

OrderProcessor::~OrderProcessor() = default;

OrderId OrderProcessor::submit(
    std::string_view customer_id,
    std::vector<OrderItem> items
) {
    assert(!items.empty());

    Money total = std::accumulate(
        items.begin(), items.end(), Money{0},
        [](Money sum, const OrderItem& item) {
            return Money{sum.cents + item.price.cents * item.quantity};
        }
    );

    auto order_id = impl_->db.create_order(
        std::string(customer_id), std::move(items), total
    );

    impl_->payments.charge(customer_id, total);
    impl_->notifications.send(
        customer_id,
        fmt::format("Order {} confirmed: ${:.2f}",
                    order_id.value, total.cents / 100.0)
    );

    if (impl_->on_completed_cb) {
        impl_->on_completed_cb(order_id);
    }

    return order_id;
}

void OrderProcessor::on_completed(Callback cb) {
    impl_->on_completed_cb = std::move(cb);
}

} // namespace myproject


// === WHY OWN HEADER FIRST MATTERS ===
//
// Suppose order_processor.h forgot to #include <string>.
// If foo.cpp has:
//   #include <string>                    // Happens to be first
//   #include "order_processor.h"         // Works! (string already included)
//
// But order_processor.cpp has:
//   #include "order_processor.h"         // FIRST → compilation error!
//   #include <string>
//
// The error surfaces immediately in the RIGHT file.


// === TRANSITIVE INCLUDE DANGER ===
//
// version 1: database.h includes <unordered_map>
// Your code uses std::unordered_map but only includes "database.h"
//
// version 2: database.h refactored, no longer includes <unordered_map>
// Your code BREAKS — you relied on a transitive include.
//
// Fix: always include what YOU use directly.


// === CI ENFORCEMENT ===
// Use include-what-you-use (IWYU) tool:
//   include-what-you-use -Xiwyu --mapping_file=iwyu.imp order_processor.cpp
// Or clang-tidy:
//   clang-tidy --checks='misc-include-cleaner' order_processor.cpp
```
